text:
The TALSPEAK process (Trivalent Actinide Lanthanide Separation with Phosphorus-reagent Extraction from Aqueous Komplexes) was developed at Oak Ridge National Laboratory in the 1960s by Weaver and Kappelmann as an alternative to the LiCl-based TRAMEX process. The process selects trivalent lanthanides for extraction into an organic phase while retaining trivalent actinides in an aqueous phase with the aid of aminopolycarboxylate complexants. The well-established TALSPEAK process using the benchmark HDEHP in an aromatic diluent with a concentrated lactate buffer and DTPA is the current global reference for advanced 4f/5f separation.

Current ionic-liquid-mediated systems have been shown to be highly efficient in practice for a number of pairs in that the separation factor required for extraction is provided by a single stage with factors of up to about 100 observed for what is arguably the most difficult of the lanthanide pairs, Am³⁺ and Nd³⁺. However, this does not mean that the systems are ready for exploitation on a commercial scale. The necessity to maintain pH in very tightly defined conditions and slow phase transfer has imposed very high lactate buffer concentrations of 0.5–2.0 M over the past decade, giving plenty of time to develop the systems into a fully funded piece of work that could be devoted to uncovering at the molecular level the reason for these constraints.

Recent advances in TALSPEAK chemistry Recent developments in the TALSPEAK process have advanced significantly beyond the empirical level and involve a more thorough understanding of the thermodynamics and kinetics of the system. Results discussed include lactic acid partition into the organic phase being the reason for the very sensitive pH response observed with the process, polynuclear mixed-ligand complexes being formed at high metal loadings in the aqueous phase and lactate being involved in the phase transfer of the actinides via certain aqueous species. Evolution of the “Advanced TALSPEAK” concepts which utilize more basic phosphonic acids and weaker holdback reagents to alleviate some of the more annoying aspects of the process is also discussed.
explanation: Contains dense, domain-specific chemistry content with some awkward phrasing and a duplicated phrase, which can be a hint of AI-generation, though the technical detail is plausible for human authors.
